reason: Natural souring agent and preservative.
simple_explanation: An acid naturally found in citrus fruits like lemons and limes. It is used in foods to add a tart or sour taste and acts as a natural preservative to keep food fresh.

reason: Synthetic chemical preservative. Can form benzene when combined with Vitamin C.
simple_explanation: A chemical preservative made in a lab to stop mold and bacteria from growing in acidic foods like sodas and pickles. It helps extend shelf life.

reason: Standard food preservative to prevent mold.
simple_explanation: A lab-made salt that stops mold and yeast from growing in cheese, wine, and yogurt. It breaks down naturally in our body just like fatty acids.

common_alias_names: MSG, INS 621, E621, Glutamate, Monosodium Glutamate
reason: Flavor enhancer. May cause sensitivity or headaches in some individuals.
simple_explanation: A sodium salt of a common amino acid. It adds a savory, meaty flavor (umami) to foods. It occurs naturally in cheese and tomatoes but is added to chips and soups to boost taste.

reason: Emulsifier extracted from soybeans.
simple_explanation: A natural fat extracted from soybeans. It acts as an emulsifier, which helps mix water and oil together so ingredients do not separate in chocolate or salad dressings.

reason: Natural thickener from guar beans.
simple_explanation: A natural powder made from guar beans. It is used to thicken foods and make textures smooth, like in ice creams and sauces.

reason: Thickener created by fermenting sugar.
simple_explanation: A natural thickener made by fermenting sugars with a specific bacteria. It gives a thick, uniform texture to salad dressings and gluten-free baking.

reason: Thickener from seaweed. May cause mild digestive inflammation in sensitive people.
simple_explanation: A gel extracted from red seaweed. It is used to thicken and stabilize dairy and plant-based milks, but it can occasionally cause stomach upset in some people.

reason: Artificial sweetener. Contains phenylalanine (hazardous for PKU patients).
simple_explanation: An artificial sweetener made in a lab. It is 200 times sweeter than sugar and used in diet sodas. It provides a sweet taste without calories, but should be avoided by people with the rare genetic condition PKU.

common_alias_names: Acesulfame K, INS 950, E950, Acesulfame Potassium
reason: Artificial zero-calorie sweetener.
simple_explanation: A calorie-free sugar substitute made in a lab. It is 200 times sweeter than sugar and is often blended with other sweeteners to mask bitter aftertastes in diet sodas.

reason: Highly processed sweetener. Strongly linked to obesity and diabetes.
simple_explanation: A highly sweet syrup made from corn starch. It is cheap and heavily used in processed foods, but eating too much of it can lead to weight gain, liver fat, and diabetes.

reason: Synthetic yellow food dye. Linked to hyperactivity in children.
simple_explanation: A bright yellow artificial food coloring made from petroleum. It is used in candies and sodas. In some children, it has been linked to hyperactivity and mild allergic reactions.

common_alias_names: INS 110, E110, Yellow 6, Sunset Yellow FCF
reason: Synthetic orange food dye. Linked to hyperactivity in children.
simple_explanation: An artificial orange food coloring. Used to make snacks and sodas look bright, but regulated or restricted in some countries due to potential hyperactivity links in kids.

common_alias_names: INS 133, E133, Blue 1, Brilliant Blue FCF
reason: Synthetic blue food dye.
simple_explanation: A bright blue artificial food coloring made from petroleum. Used in blue candies, drinks, and ice creams. Regulated in quantity as it does not occur in nature.

reason: Ammonia-sulfite processed coloring. Contains trace 4-MEI in some classes.
simple_explanation: A dark brown food coloring made by heating carbohydrates (sugars) under controlled chemistry. Class III and IV varieties use ammonia or sulfites and are regulated in soft drinks.

reason: High in saturated fats. Environmental concerns.
simple_explanation: A vegetable oil extracted from palm fruit. It is highly saturated, making it semi-solid at room temp and perfect for crispy cookies and long shelf life, but it raises LDL cholesterol.

common_alias_names: Hydrogenated Vegetable Oil, Vanaspati, Trans Fat
reason: Contains trans fats which raise harmful cholesterol and lower good cholesterol.
simple_explanation: Vegetable oil that has been chemically altered with hydrogen to turn it from liquid to solid. It creates trans fats, which are very bad for heart health and block arteries.

reason: High glycemic index starch derivative. Spikes blood sugar rapidly.
simple_explanation: A processed white powder made from starch (like corn or rice). It is used as a filler or thickener in soups and snacks. It spikes blood sugar even faster than regular sugar.

reason: Synthetic emulsifier. May disrupt gut microbiome.
simple_explanation: A synthetic emulsifier used to keep oils and water mixed in ice cream and cosmetics. Some studies suggest it might irritate the gut lining in sensitive stomachs.

reason: Sulfite preservative. Can trigger severe asthma in sensitive people.
simple_explanation: A chemical preservative and antioxidant used to prevent browning in dried fruits and juices. It releases sulfur dioxide, which can trigger severe allergic reactions in asthmatics.

reason: Synthetic antioxidant. Classified as a suspected endocrine disruptor and carcinogen.
simple_explanation: A synthetic chemical added to fatty foods like chips and vegetable oils to stop them from going rancid. It is regulated because it is a suspected hormone disruptor.

reason: Synthetic antioxidant. Suspected endocrine disruptor.
simple_explanation: Similar to BHA, this is a lab-made preservative used to keep fats and cereals fresh. Some animal studies suggest high doses might impact hormones.

reason: Synthetic food preservative for fats.
simple_explanation: A synthetic preservative used to prevent oxidation and spoilage in oils, instant noodles, and frozen foods. Restricted to very low concentrations globally.

reason: Synthetic red food dye. Linked to hyperactivity.
simple_explanation: A bright red food coloring made from petroleum. Widely used in candies, condiments, and soft drinks, but linked to hyperactivity in sensitive children.

reason: Dairy protein byproduct.
simple_explanation: A protein-rich powder filtered from liquid whey, which is a byproduct of cheese-making. Great for muscle building, but contains dairy/lactose.

common_alias_names: Maida, Refined Wheat Flour, Wheat Flour, Atta
reason: Staple grain flour.
simple_explanation: Flour milled from wheat grains. Refined wheat flour (maida) has the outer fiber and germ removed, leaving mostly starch, which digests very quickly.

reason: Dried milk components.
simple_explanation: The solid components left after removing water from milk (like proteins, lactose, and minerals). Used to enrich texture in chocolates and milk biscuits.

reason: Natural fat from cocoa beans.
simple_explanation: The natural creamy fat pressed out of cacao beans. It is what makes chocolate melt smoothly in the mouth and is highly stable.

reason: Chlorinated artificial sweetener.
simple_explanation: A zero-calorie sweetener made by chemically modifying sugar with chlorine so our body cannot digest it. It is 600 times sweeter than sugar and used in sugar-free snacks.

reason: Sugar alcohol sweetener.
simple_explanation: A natural sugar alcohol sweetener found in small amounts in fruits. It has almost zero calories, does not spike blood sugar, and is friendly on teeth.

reason: Acidity regulator from citric acid.
simple_explanation: The sodium salt of citric acid. It acts as a buffer or souring agent in club sodas and gelatin desserts to control acidity and add a salty-sour punch.

reason: Natural soluble fiber from fruit peels.
simple_explanation: A natural soluble fiber found in fruit peels (especially apples and oranges). It is used as a gelling agent to thicken jams, jellies, and fruit spreads.

reason: Acidifier in sodas. Excess intake can lower bone density.
simple_explanation: A strong chemical acid added to cola drinks to give them a sharp, tangy taste. High consumption can interfere with calcium absorption and affect bone strength.

reason: Natural mineral source and color.
simple_explanation: A natural mineral source of calcium (like chalk or limestone). Used in foods as an anticaking agent, white coloring, or calcium supplement.

common_alias_names: INS 466, E466, Cellulose Gum, Sodium Carboxymethyl Cellulose
reason: Thickener derived from plant fiber.
simple_explanation: A thickener made by chemically treating natural plant cellulose fiber. It creates a creamy body in milkshakes and ice creams.

reason: Sugar alcohol sweetener. Can cause laxative effects in high doses.
simple_explanation: A sugar alcohol used as a low-calorie sweetener and moisture-holder in sugar-free gums. It is digested slowly, but eating too much can cause gas or diarrhea.

reason: Humectant and sweetener.
simple_explanation: A natural liquid sweet alcohol used to keep foods moist and soft, such as in energy bars and cake frostings. It is safe and digests like other carbohydrates.

reason: Natural savory flavor enhancer.
simple_explanation: A savory paste made by extracting the natural glutamates from yeast cells. It is used to add a rich savory flavor (like MSG) to chips and spreads.

reason: Processed flavor enhancer. Contains bound MSG.
simple_explanation: A savory flavoring powder made by boiling soy, corn, or wheat proteins in acid to break them down. It contains natural MSG and is used to add meat-like flavor.

reason: Mold inhibitor used in breads.
simple_explanation: A calcium salt made in a lab that is highly effective at stopping mold and bacteria from spoiling sliced bread and baked goods, keeping them fresh longer.

reason: Concentrated artificial vanilla flavor.
simple_explanation: An artificial vanilla flavoring agent made in a lab. It smells and tastes like vanilla but is 3 times stronger than natural vanillin, so very tiny amounts are used.

reason: Simple corn sugar. Spikes blood sugar rapidly.
simple_explanation: A simple sugar chemically identical to blood glucose. Milled from corn starch, it is highly sweet and quickly absorbed by the body, raising blood sugar levels rapidly.

reason: Sweetener syrup that holds moisture.
simple_explanation: A liquid mixture of glucose and fructose made by breaking down regular table sugar. It is sweeter than sugar, holds moisture, and keeps cookies soft.

reason: Natural souring agent found in apples.
simple_explanation: An organic acid naturally found in apples and other tart fruits. It is used to add a sharp, sour fruit flavor to candies, drinks, and snacks.
